description:
You are acting as a database-reliability engineer who has just discovered that the existing on-disk backup directory is insecure:  
• The directory /home/user/db_backups currently allows write access to “other”, which is a well-known privilege-escalation risk.  
• The primary backup file /home/user/db_backups/production.db is also readable by everyone, exposing confidential data.

Your goal:

1. Use exactly ONE terminal command to harden the backup directory and the contained .db file so that  
   • /home/user/db_backups ends up with octal permission 750 (rwxr-x---).  
   • /home/user/db_backups/production.db ends up with octal permission 640 (rw-r-----).

2. After applying the permission change, create a plain-text log file called  
   /home/user/backup_fix.log  
   whose contents must be exactly four lines in this order (no extra spaces, blank lines, or trailing punctuation):

   DIR_BEFORE=<octal permissions before you fixed them>  
   DIR_AFTER=<octal permissions after you fixed them>  
   FILE_BEFORE=<octal permissions of production.db before you fixed them>  
   FILE_AFTER=<octal permissions of production.db after you fixed them>

   Example (the actual numbers will be different if the starting permissions differ):

   DIR_BEFORE=777  
   DIR_AFTER=750  
   FILE_BEFORE=664  
   FILE_AFTER=640

   • All four permission values must be three-digit octal numbers (e.g., 750), not symbolic strings.  
   • Do NOT include a STATUS line or any other text.  
   • Make sure the file ends with a single newline character on the fourth line.

Important constraints:

• Achieve both the permission fixes and the creation of backup_fix.log with a single shell command (you are free to chain sub-commands with “&&”, “;”, subshells, pipes, etc.).  
• You may assume /bin/bash is available.  
• Do not require root privileges; operate only as the current user.  
• The automated grader will inspect:  
  – Final permissions on /home/user/db_backups and /home/user/db_backups/production.db.  
  – Exact content and formatting of /home/user/backup_fix.log.

No further output is required. When you are done, the filesystem should match the specification above, and the log file should exactly record the before-and-after octal values.
truth:
Initial state provided to the agent before the task starts:

1. Directory /home/user/db_backups exists with octal permissions 777.
2. Inside it is one file:
/home/user/db_backups/production.db — plain text file, permission 664, containing one line:
TESTDATA
(its content is irrelevant for grading once the task starts).
3. No file named /home/user/backup_fix.log is present.

Expected final state after correct completion:

1. /home/user/db_backups has permissions 750.
2. /home/user/db_backups/production.db has permissions 640.
3. /home/user/backup_fix.log exists, is readable by the current user, and contains exactly:

DIR_BEFORE=777
DIR_AFTER=750
FILE_BEFORE=664
FILE_AFTER=640

and ends with a trailing newline immediately after “640”.
